narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.80—well above the typical 3.21 average—your law school application stood out in several ways, including your middle-family-income background and plans to attend full-time like most students. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 55.6% chance that you would fail it, tipping the scales toward rejection to minimize the risk of poor bar passage rates. This prediction hinged on just a few key factors from your profile, with your LSAT score of 27—far below the average of 36.7—emerging as the single biggest influence, strongly signaling to the model a higher risk of bar failure. Next in importance was your undergraduate GPA, which pushed in the opposite direction by suggesting you have the academic strength to succeed and pass the exam. Rounding out the top influences, your Year 3 school decile ranking of 4—lower than the average 5.53—nudged the prediction slightly toward failure, indicating relatively weaker performance in that stage compared to peers. These elements combined to outweigh your other strengths, like that impressive GPA, in the model's eyes. While your full-time status aligns with the vast majority of students (over 92%), it didn't sway the outcome much here. Many admitted students have LSAT scores closer to or above the norm, paired with consistently higher decile rankings throughout school, which helps them clear the bar exam threshold more reliably. Your situation isn't uncommon for applicants with uneven test performance despite strong college grades, but it placed you just on the riskier side of the line. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score toward the average, as it carries the most weight; strengthening your later academic deciles through additional coursework could also help. Gathering stronger letters of recommendation or bar prep experience might further demonstrate your potential in a future application. With targeted improvements, especially on that key test score, you could flip this prediction and strengthen your candidacy significantly.
